did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures, specifically ResNet50 and VGG16, to classify skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer detection. The study employed deep learning techniques including transfer learning, fine-tuning, and data augmentation to obtain classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing the performance and efficiency of two specific CNN architectures (VGG16 and ResNet50) on the HAM10000 dataset. It details the methodology including transfer learning, fine-tuning, and data augmentation, and reports specific quantitative metrics such as accuracy (82.14% for ResNet50, 71.32% for VGG16 in Indonesian abstract; 80.96% and 74.85% in English abstract), precision, and F1-score. The structure includes standard research sections like Abstract, Introduction, Methods, Results, and Conclusion, indicating it is an original study rather than a review.

title: PERBANDINGAN KINERJA DAN EFISIENSI ARSITEKTUR CNN VGG-16 DAN RESNET50 DALAM KANKER KULIT DATASET HAM10000
journal: JITET (Jurnal Informatika dan Teknik Elektro Terapan)
brief_summary: This study compared the performance and efficiency of two Convolutional Neural Network architectures, ResNet50 and VGG16, for classifying skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset. Both models were trained using transfer learning with ImageNet weights, fine-tuning, and data augmentation techniques. The evaluation focused on accuracy, precision, and F1-score, revealing that ResNet50 achieved superior overall performance with an accuracy of 82.14% (Indonesian abstract) or 80.96% (English abstract) compared to VGG16's 71.32% or 74.85%. While ResNet50 demonstrated better generalization and higher scores in precision and F1-score, both models struggled with minority classes like melanoma due to dataset imbalance. The results indicate that ResNet50 is a more reliable architecture for skin cancer detection despite requiring longer training times than VGG16.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset using CNN architectures (ResNet50 and VGG16). The models predict discrete class labels by selecting the class with the highest probability (argmax) from the output probabilities. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for each of the seven lesion classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: A global benchmark dataset for dermatology-based image studies, comprising over 10,000 dermatoscopic images representing various types of skin lesions, including benign and malignant tumors such as melanoma, nevi, and keratosis. The dataset was obtained from Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet50 and VGG16 models with data augmentation (rotation, shifting, zoom, lighting changes, horizontal flipping) applied to the training subset.
use.validation: Used for validation with 15% of the data split via stratified splitting; no augmentation was applied.
use.testing: Used for testing with 15% of the data split via stratified splitting; no augmentation was applied. The testing set consisted of 1503 images.
use.other: The dataset was preprocessed by mapping image file locations, filtering valid images, resizing to 64x64 pixels, and normalizing using preprocess_input.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The comparison between ResNet50 and VGG16 was based on descriptive statistics of performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices.

HAM10000.notes: ResNet50 achieved higher accuracy than VGG16. Both models struggled with minority classes (melanoma, dermatofibroma) due to dataset imbalance.

HAM10000_Comparison_Notes.metric: Weighted Average (from text description of confusion matrix analysis)
HAM10000_Comparison_Notes.notes: Textual analysis of confusion matrices indicated ResNet50 had superior generalization. Specific recall improvements for ResNet50 on minority classes: Melanoma (0.60 vs 0.30 for VGG16) and Dermatofibroma (0.42 vs 0.08 for VGG16).

ResNet50.key_features: Residual connections (shortcut connections) to address degradation problem and vanishing gradient; bottleneck blocks (1x1, 3x3, 1x1)

VGG16.composition: 13 convolutional layers, 5 pooling layers, 3 fully connected layers
VGG16.convolution_details: 3x3 filters with ReLU activation; max pooling with 2x2 kernel
VGG16.key_features: Small filter size (3x3) for deep feature extraction

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (standard Keras/TensorFlow defaults implied)

classification_or_output_head.output_mechanism: Argmax of output probabilities for multi-class classification

preprocessing_techniques: Mapping image file locations to match image_id with file paths
Image filtering to ensure only valid and available image files are used
Stratified data splitting into 75% training, 15% validation, and 15% testing sets
Data augmentation on training data including rotation, shifting, zoom, lighting changes, and horizontal flipping
Data normalization using preprocess_input() from Keras to match ImageNet standard

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random horizontal shift up to 12.5% of total width (width_shift_range=0.125).
Random vertical shift up to 12.5% of total height (height_shift_range=0.125).

conclusions: ResNet50 demonstrated superior overall performance compared to VGG16, achieving a testing accuracy of 82.14% (Indonesian abstract) or 80.96% (English abstract), whereas VGG16 achieved 71.32% or 74.85%.
ResNet50 showed better generalization capability and higher scores in precision and F1-score compared to VGG16.
ResNet50 outperformed classical classification methods such as Weighted K-NN, M-SVM, and Naïve Bayes reported in previous studies.
VGG16 was computationally lighter and performed well on majority classes (e.g., Melanocytic nevi), but suffered significant performance drops on minority classes.
Both models struggled with minority classes, specifically melanoma and dermatofibroma, due to class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset.
Although ResNet50 required longer training times than VGG16, the performance improvement was considered proportional to the additional computational cost.
The study highlights the necessity for further approaches, such as data balancing and augmentation, to enhance performance on minority classes.
The proposed ResNet50-based approach holds potential for developing AI-based skin cancer detection applications to assist medical professionals in accelerating diagnoses.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (HAM10000) with significant class imbalance (e.g., 1006 samples for Melanocytic nevi vs. 17 for Dermatofibroma), leading to poor performance on minority classes like melanoma and dermatofibroma, which limits generalizability.
No external validation was performed; results are confined to the HAM10000 testing split, raising concerns about clinical applicability to diverse populations or different imaging devices.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (p-values) for the performance differences between ResNet50 and VGG16, relying solely on descriptive metrics.

ResNet50 achieved an accuracy of 82.14% (Indonesian abstract) or 80.96% (English abstract), which is relatively low for a critical diagnostic task, indicating that the model is not yet ready for standalone clinical deployment without further improvement.
The input image resolution was reduced to 64x64 pixels, which may discard fine-grained dermatological features necessary for accurate diagnosis of subtle lesions.
Hyperparameters such as learning rate, specific dropout rates, and loss function are not explicitly reported, limiting the reproducibility of the study.

The comparison with classical methods (Weighted K-NN, M-SVM, Naïve Bayes) references previous studies rather than re-running them on the same data split, making direct performance comparisons potentially invalid due to differing experimental conditions.
Data leakage risk is mitigated by applying data augmentation only to the training set and not to validation/testing sets, but the stratified split details (random seed) are not provided.
